Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly just go with the flow and study whenever I felt like it, which wasn't often. Sometimes I'd binge-watch course videos for a few hours when I got into it, but then I wouldn't touch anything for days. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so sitting down to read or watch lectures just doesn't come naturally to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks without doing much, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. Regular schedules aren't really my thing - I've always been someone who works in bursts when the pressure hits. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I got a notification about something being due, or because I was bored and figured I should at least check what was happening. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pop up and I'd think 'okay, this could be interesting.' But it wasn't like I woke up excited to check the LMS or anything.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. There were a few weeks where I was logging in more because the gamified stuff made it feel less like a chore, but I wouldn't call it a routine. I'd watch a bunch of videos in one sitting and then disappear for a while.
Q5: The videos were actually decent because I could watch them while doing other things. The gamified tasks were the most useful honestly - they made me actually want to participate because there was something to achieve. When learning feels like a game, I'm way more likely to engage with it.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. Posting online just feels awkward to me, and I never knew what to say that would be worth contributing. I also skipped a lot of the readings because they were so dry. If something wasn't interactive or visual, I probably didn't bother with it.
Q7: If it felt hands-on or had some kind of reward or progress tracker, I was more interested. The gamified elements made things feel worth it because I could see myself making progress. Anything that was just 'read this and absorb it' felt like a waste of time to me.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt more appealing weirdly enough. Required activities felt like obligations, which made me want to avoid them. Optional stuff felt like my choice, so when I did engage with it, I was more invested. But I also used 'optional' as an excuse to skip things when I wasn't feeling it.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the videos and materials to my phone so I could study without being connected. I'd watch lectures while commuting or lying in bed. I also used YouTube to find explanations that clicked better with how I learn - more visual and practical demonstrations.
Q10: There were times I'd download everything in one session and then study from those files for days without logging back in. Or I'd be working through problems on paper, practicing concepts physically, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere. The LMS only sees when I'm online, not when I'm actually learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download videos and PDFs in one go, then work through them over the next week or two without touching the platform. It was easier than constantly logging in, and I could study on my own terms. My watch time probably looks high because I'd leave videos playing, but the real studying happened offline.
Q12: Around midterms I just completely checked out for a while. I was stressed about other stuff in life, and the motivation to engage just wasn't there. When things feel overwhelming, I tend to shut down rather than push through. But I bounced back eventually - I'm pretty resilient like that.
Q13: Yeah, when I had too much going on, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. If I was tired or confused about the material, I'd just close the tab and tell myself I'd deal with it later. Competing responsibilities definitely won out over coursework most of the time.
Q14: Oh for sure. Sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was technically 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. It's like, I know it's important, but the motivation just isn't there until the last minute. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure anyway. Sometimes I avoided things because I didn't fully understand them and didn't want to face that.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ignore everything until a deadline was close, then suddenly become very active. The LMS probably shows these spikes of activity right before due dates. Without deadlines, I honestly wouldn't have done much at all.
Q17: Not really. I didn't pay much attention to feedback because by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If I got a bad grade, I'd feel annoyed for a bit but it didn't change how I approached the next thing. Feedback felt like it came too late to matter.
Q18: The structure was okay but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. Having everything available at once meant I could procrastinate forever. The gamified elements helped a bit because they gave me smaller goals to hit, but overall the course structure didn't force me into any rhythm.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they gave me something concrete to work toward. But having so many online courses at once made it hard to care about any single one. The lack of in-person interaction made everything feel disconnected and easy to ignore.
Q20: That's kind of me honestly. I'd log in, watch videos, click around, but when it came to actually submitting work, I just couldn't bring myself to do it. Sometimes you're present but not productive. The LMS activity might show curiosity or attempts to engage, but that doesn't mean assignments get done.
Q21: Yeah, that makes sense to me. Someone could be studying effectively offline and only pop into the LMS to submit things or grab what they need. I did okay on my exam even though my attendance and assignment completion were low because I learned in my own way, just not through the platform.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just zoning out. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the screen, and whether you're actually motivated or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just completing tasks for the sake of it. It's about being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement happens when something feels relevant or interesting, not when I'm just checking boxes.
Q24: The data wouldn't show that I actually did learn stuff, just not in the ways the system tracks. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my resilience in bouncing back after rough patches, or the fact that gamified elements genuinely helped my mood and motivation. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
